sentences.text: Most of the time, it was wonderful.
It was-- it was good, actually.
My best friends was a-- a girl by the name of Jalinka and her brother, Diesjek.
They were our neighbors.
I used to go to their place, to help them, you know, do their Christmas tree.
And at the Jewish holidays, at Rosh Hashanah and, and Purim, for instance, they would come over to our house.
And I used to sleep over there.
And we were really good friends.
And that, too, came to an end.
And it came to an end, really, that changed my life.
It was one of those holidays, you know.
It was Easter time, and it was the last day of school before the Easter break.
And I was going home, minding my own business, quite happy to, to get home.
And halfway through it, I got stopped by a group of Gentile kids that I knew, including Diesjek and Jalinka.
And they beat the hell out of me. 'You killed Jesus Christ.' And I kept hearing this.
And I barely got home.
I was-- you know, I had to-- I think my mother called a doctor, to patch me up, because there-- I was-- blood coming from my nose, from my ears.
And they really did a number on me.
And I was quite upset with my parents.
I says, why did we kill Jesus Christ?
And this is how I was introduced to, to anti-Semitism.
So I was in grade two when this happened, shortly before the war, 1938.
And our relationship after that was never the same.
It was brought home to me, you're a Jewish kid.
And I remember my brothers, you know, took a-- the inside of a, the tube, you know, from a bicycle.
And they made a sling for me.
And they took me out in the, in the field there and showed me how to use this sling.
I was pretty good at it.
And anybody that approached me, you know, I just put my weapon in front of me, and, and-- For the longest time after this, you know, my brothers would shadow me.
They would just follow me and make sure that no harm came to me.
That, that was the only incident.
And it sort of wisened me up, and I had to be on my guard.
And it was a very costly lesson to me.
Because it sort of shattered my, my thinking and my relationship with these two, for instance-- this brother and sister, which I, I held kind of dearly.
And I was a bit disillusioned, not  'a bit,' but a lot disillusioned.
And-- but it brought my Jewishness home to me.
And I realized that we were different.
Yes, I did, but guardedly.
I, you know, I didn't let my guard down.
I was always on the lookout.
You want to be a friend-- friends?
Fine.
But it was more or less on my terms.
And that was a shame, because, before that, I gave it all, you know.
After that, when they invited me over, I never slept over, after that.
My trust had disappeared.
From what I found out-- I looked it up at Yad Vashem-- and what I remember reading about it, I think there were some-- almost about 4,000 Jewish people in Skarzysko.
And the proportion of that with large families, of course, there were some 1,400 or 1,500 children, you know, under-- under the age of 15.
I think the percentage of the ones that survived are minute.
I think there's three or four of us that, that survived.
Of course, when we finished the public school, we went-- I went to cheder.
And that was a different kind of life, again.
At the public school, again, after this incident, I was on my guard.
And when they had the Lord's Prayer-- they had more than the Lord's Prayer.
They had a religious service.
And so, us Jewish kids would be excused.
And we would go out for three quarters of an hour and play on the-- in the hallways.
Because we didn't want to-- you know, we had this-- at least that privilege, to let us out.
And don't forget, it was mainly Catholic.
And that's the reason I got beaten up.
Because the liturgy on Sunday school when they went was very, very [NON-ENGLISH], you know.
It was-- very, very anti-Semitic.
And you know, only till recently it was still in there.
Not so many years ago, it was still-- And, to this day, I think some of these Eastern countries are still-- you know, I was, I was shattered when I saw the for instance.
And he interviewed these Poles that-- the Jews have-- have disappeared from Poland!
And, and you see still the, the imprint of the mezuzah in the doors.
And they talk about the Jews-- oh, how wonderful that they disappeared, that they killed them.
They should have killed all of them.
You would think that, after all these years, that there would be a little bit of, of feeling left in the Poles, and say-- and a little bit of compassion.
But it's not there.
After school, there was a violin-- a violin-- in our house.
And I think my older-- my two brothers were pretty good at  it.
And I think I started doing this but didn't get anywhere because of the impending war, and so on.
So, as I remember-- and then, of course, as far as physical activity is concerned, oh, yes we did.
I played soccer, you know, we called it 'football,' at the time.
And we were very active, doing, you know, kid's games and what have you.
There was no basketball.
Soccer was the main thing, so we played that.
But I remember my brothers were involved in organized sports, but not me.
I was just the kid that was just getting along with them and sometimes unwanted and so on.
So.
Movies, I wasn't allowed to see.
I remember my brothers going-- the Tarzan movies were very much in, in style then.
And my brother Abram was very good.
And he used to come-- when he came home after a movie, the next day, he would just tell me the whole movie, you know, blow-by-blow what was happening.
Oh, god, I couldn't wait!
I wanted to get old enough so I can go.
I think on two occasions I remember that I was allowed to go to movies.
Because they are usually in the evening.
And I don't think there were any matinees, because of course, my mum thought that my going to sleep was more important than going to see a Tarzan movie.
My mother and father, I think-- we went to one place, and I can't remember the name.
But it was considered a resort.
Skarzysko had quite a few factories.
And this one-- the air was clear, and there were lakes and-- and wooded areas to play.
And I remember at one time spending the time with them, just the three of us.
And I particularly remember the one walk that we had.
And I was just playing in this lake.
And they were just walking, hand-in-hand and, I guess, away from the rest of the family.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's funny, I hadn't thought about this for many, many years, and I can just see it.
It doesn't seem like I was there.
It's just that I can see the little boy there.
And obviously, they must have taken me along because my brothers and sister were probably old enough to look after themselves.
And of course the baby got spoiled.
So I got to go with them.
But as a unit, together?
No, I remember my brothers going off on various excursions, through the organizations, the-- through the organizations I mentioned before.
The Zionist organizations were very much alive and very, very active.
And I think it was a matter of time.
If the war had started, say, another, say, two years later than it did, I'm confident that my brothers might have survived.
Because they might have gone to Palestine at the time.
Aliyah was very much, you know, talked about.
And people were very interested in it.
I remember my father saying you know, he was a bit concerned that they would leave home, you know, and sort of split up the family.
That was the only negative about it.

But I think generally it was recognized in Poland that with the anti-Semitism and all-- and we used to get reports about Palestine and what some of them that left for Palestine, when they wrote home letters, they were glowing about having their own place to-- to-- and, of course, to do-- to become farmers, that was a novel idea, too.
And-- oh, yeah, another name that comes to mind is Ze'ev Jabotinsky.
You know, he was-- his style of life and what he was trying to do in Palestine at the time was very attractive to young people.
You know, he was out there and showing them how to use guns and how to defend themselves.
Because there was always problems with the Arabs.
My family always talked about it.
They brought home some of these incidents.
My older brother Motel, the one next to the-- my older brother, was going away to a special school in a place called Radom.
And I remember him relaying incidents of anti-Semitism.
It was all around us, it was there all the time.
I just experienced it on a very, very small scale.
My experience was as a child.
But it started there.
So I can imagine what their experiences were as they were growing up.
And I would get these feedbacks, you know, at the supper table, talking about-- but it was an occurrence that was a daily occurrence.
And-- and people learned to live with it.
Yes, there was some talk about leaving.
And-- but we were going to leave because I had an aunt-- two aunts, actually-- that left for France.
And they used to come and visit.
One was Pola, and, oh, gosh, the other one just escaped me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Two sisters that were my, my father's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sisters.
And so they left, and they would come home.
I remember one of them, I remember a nice fur coat that she wore.
And she was telling how much easier and how much better Jewish life was in, in, in France.
So there was consideration of, of doing that.
I wish we had.
Although the Jews in France didn't fare that well during the war.
Were there any early warning signs before the war?
Well, anti-Semitism was-- was everywhere, you know.
And of course, in 1938-- when I get back to 1938 and '39, before the war broke out-- we-- there were German Jews that were coming.
You know, after Kristallnacht, there were-- some of them were coming into Poland.
And, of course, they was-- they were telling us what-- what was about to happen and so on.
So there was this talk.
Yes, they did.
And so did-- some of the-- the neighbors, as I told you before, they would come.
You know, we would gather for all the happy occasions.
They also came to our house because my father was quite good at giving advice.
So they came for him.
He was sort of looked up-- excuse me-- in the community.
[DRINKS]
So they would come to Chil Waisman for advice.
They would come to him to talk things over.
So I remember when they would come and express their fears.
They says, did you hear about what's happening in, in, in Germany?
And if, if the war breaks out, and they win, and they come into Poland, they want to kill Jews, left and right!
And I remember so distinctly my father, you know, saying, you crazy?
You know, they have-- look at that-- they have Beethoven, they have Schubert, they have-- at that time, he was mentioning Schuler, you know, because he liked to read a lot.
Anyway, Schuler and Heine.
Oh-- all these poets and these composers.
And-- he says, Germany, they're so advanced of all the-- of all of Europe, of all the world, he was saying.
What are you worried about?
They're-- How did this affect you--
--they're civilized--
Well you know, it comes back to me.
Because I listened to it.
And when I listened to my father, he made it so simple.
And he assured our neighbors, he assured all of us, that life is beautiful, and that don't worry, just concentrate on becoming a mensch, on going and study and become a mensch.
And he-- he was-- he taught us all you know, the integrity to-- to be good to the next human being.
He was a wonderful human being.
And so he believed in humanity.
To him, the fears that were expressed-- He was, he was absolutely surprised and amazed that-- that Kristallnacht in '38 occurred.
He says, people went mad.
It's-- it's, it's a once in a lifetime thing.
Something like this will not repeat itself.
Civilized people!
Civilized people, learned people!
They're not going to just kill us.
What are you talking about?
And so the neighbors left, feeling good, you know, feeling happy.
Yeah, Chil Waisman says it's not going to happen.
Don't worry about it.
And you know, you want to hear good things.
You don't want to believe in, in the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the bad side of, of people.
You-- you know, when you see, you hear something awful happening-- no.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You like to justify it.
You like to, to rationalize-- I think that's a better word.
And my father was very good in rationalizing.
Civilized and this, and that.
It's not going to happen.

So, now-- so you see, what I'm telling you-- all this love and the security that I felt at home and the surrounding around me, and, and what-- the things that my father used to tell about the situation in Poland, I felt secure.
I felt wonderful.
I went on going to school, I went on playing.
And if something happens, my family's going to look after me.
September-- I think it was the 7th or 8th of September-- but it was a beautiful, wonderful day, the sun was shining.
It was Sunday, because I didn't go to school.
I think I was going to cheder in the morning, and that was in the afternoon.
I was playing outside.
It was sort of an Indian-summer day.
You know, you could almost sense the cool breeze, before the sun came out.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we saw planes come overhead.
And they bombed.
Bombs fell and hit one of the-- on the station-- there was a main station in Skarzysko.
So when it hit one of the cars, it had some chemicals in it.
And it just-- a bunch of black smoke just pillared out into the air.
And people started running all over the place.
And I remember my, my mother looking for me, and she found me.
And she grabbed me, and we ran quickly into a bomb shelter.
And she gave me a towel of-- soaked in water, that I held to my nose.
Because everybody was screaming, gas, gas, looking at the black smoke.
It was just chemicals, and of course, there wasn't any gas.
But this, this-- this is the day that I remember sort of shattered the peacefulness and the everyday life.
And from then on, of course, a couple days afterwards, the Germans marched in to Poland and Skarzysko.
Marched in?
I remember two soldiers, two Vermacht kids-- they must have been 18, 19-- coming in.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remembered all the tanks and the trucks and, and soldiers with the helmets and everything else.
And then things sort of calmed down.
And I remember those two soldiers coming to our door.
And they-- you know, German and Yiddish was very close together.
So we started communicating fairly easy.
And these two soldiers wanted to come in, and they wanted my mother to make them a cup of tea.
Would they please have a cup of tea?
And we were frightened, you know.
First of all, because we didn't know what to make of it.
But these soldiers walked into our house, they sat down.
My mother served them tea.
And everybody was on the edge.
And I remember that my mother put on the table-- and she used to make this wine-- you know, sweet wine, it was a kiddush wine.
And so it was on the table.
And they asked what it was, so we told them.
And my mother said, it's very good with tea.
And so they put it in their tea, and they thought it was fabulous.
And they were extremely polite, and they expressed appreciation to all of us.
And-- and then they left.
And I remember my father, afterwards, saying to some of the neighbors, I told you.
They're civilized people.
We had these two boys, and they were absolutely kind, gentle, very polite.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it just reinforced his, his opinion of a civilized nation, civilized people.
This war is not going to last long.
The civilized world is going to get together.
He had this right.
And they will not allow Nazism to, to reign.
They're here for a while, but they'll be chased out.
That was my father's opinion.
The reality, of course as we all know, was quite different.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A few days afterwards, we suddenly noticed different uniforms, SS marked on them.
Didn't know what was SS.
And then some other group with SA on it, a brown uniform.
Each uniform, we learned, as the war went on, represented different brutalities.
The SS, the first recollection that I have-- I was you know, eight and a half at the time-- the roundups.
They would come, unload the truck, and soldiers would be coming up with bayonets and machine guns, rounding up people on the streets, Jews and non-Jews alike.
After a while, it was only Jews.
Grab them on the truck, and they would leave.
Some of them would come back at night, after they did a day's work.
Pretty soon, some of them wouldn't come.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The next roundups, people were running away.
I saw somebody being shot.
I ran home, told my mom, somebody was shot, killed!
I said, they were dragging him away.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so slowly, this naiveness that we had about humanity was beginning to disappear.
People were getting frantic.
People were dying.
People were really getting-- beginning to get worried.
Well, that came shortly after, you know.
I think it was a year before, of course, the ghetto was being established.
And we had, I think a day or so to just get out from the house we lived and into the ghetto.
But prior to the ghetto--
Prior to the--
There was just the roundups.
In Skarzysko, there was an ammunitions factory, by the name of HASAG.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have some details on, on that.
It was an organization-- a factory from Germany that just came over and established themself.
And they were doing munitions and so on.
So they-- there was a call out to, to the Jewish people, first, to come and work.
And they would get paid and so on.
So some of them did.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But then afterwards, it was obligatory.
Any abled men or women, if you didn't come voluntarily, they would just find you on the street and put you to work there.
Did your father go to work?
Yes, he did.
Well, we had to give it up when-- we had to-- so-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had to close the doors pretty well.
And he went, had a work permit, and went to work in the munitions factory.
And so did my brothers.
And my sister also had a job.
And she was tending cows.
A lot of the young girls were rounded up.
And they were looking after this farm that belonged to the Gestapo.
So there were-- and, as a matter of fact, I remember my mother worrying about her.
And I know where they were.
It was sort of like an hour and a half walking, where this farm was.
She cooked up some chicken soup and some chicken and some lokshen and some-- a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a part of a bit of a cholent.
And there I was, taking this to my sister.
Because I was still able to walk free and brought her this food.

And when she found out, when I got home, that the parcel was delivered safely and she has it, I can still-- you know, the smile on her face was-- of course, I didn't maybe appreciate or understood, you know, the heart of a Yiddische mama.
Her little girl had a, a meal that she made.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think it's only in one occasion that, that I did that.
So the only ones that were at home were my mum.
She would-- she refused to leave.
She wanted to look after the castle.
You know, if somebody comes home, I'm here for you.
That's-- that was-- she didn't, she didn't think about-- she could have gone to work there and maybe be safe, in that sense.
But she had the baby, which was me, at home.
And she had anybody that would come home.
And she was home.
Only-- no.
There was-- we weren't able to.
The-- the ghetto was soon established.
And they were still able to come and go, although they stayed in the camp.
So it was still not that drastic, you know.
They were able to come home and then go back.
But once the ghetto was firmly established, then it changed that.
You worked at the munitions factory.
You had barracks-- camps.
There were camps established.
And that was it.
Yes, we were.
And it was verbal, whenever he could come, he would come.
But he would stay at the munitions factory.
OK.
